content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA

AIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
100 K Street
Sacramento 95814
(916) 322-5660

February 3, 1976

A-96-305

Mr. Hugh Boyle
Western Farms
3100 So. Grand Avenue
Los Angeles, California 90007

Dear Mr. Boyle:

Your letter has been referred to me for response.

The Political Reform Act requires all state and local government agencies to adopt conflict of interest Codes during 1976. Under these Codes, the financial interests of public officials which may be materially affected by any of their decisions must be disclosed.

Persons who are unsalaried members of boards or commissions which are solely advisory need not report. However, the Fair Political Practices Commission has adopted a regulation concluding that a board is not solely advisory but has decision-making authority if:

"(A) It may make a final governmental decision;

(B) It may compel a governmental decision by any agency;

(C) Its action, recommendation, or consideration is a legal prerequisite to a final governmental decision; or

(D) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by another public official or governmental agency."

2 Cal.Adm. Code §18700(a)(1)

Mr. Hugh Boyle
February 3, 1976

Page Two

Without making a definitive determination at this
time, it appears that the Dairy Council is a decision-
making board within the meaning of the cited Commission
Regulation, 2 Cal.Adm. Code §18700(a)(1). Accordingly,
it would seem that you will be subject to the disclosure
and disqualification requirements of the Political Reform
Act.

I hope this information is helpful to you. If you
have any further questions, please call me at 322-6441.

Sincerely,


Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
Conflict of Interest Division


DS:gb

Enclosure

FOR CONSIDERATION
AT OPINION REQUEST
MEETING

JUN 6 1230PM'76

3100 SOUTH GRAND AVENUE • LOS ANGELES CALIFORNIA 90007 • RICHMOND 9-3204

Mr. Daniel I. Lowenstein
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Lowenstein

I understand decisions will be made soon regarding

financial disclosures and conflicts of interest as outlined in

the Fair Political Practices Act. As Chairman of the Board of

the Dairy Council of California, I am interested in the possible

outcome and ask for consideration of the followin points.

My an ointment to the Dairy Council Board is made

by the Director of the Dep rtment of Food and Agriculture and

the law states clearly that we are strictly advisory to him.

I feel that I am appointed because my knowledge of what the

dairy industry wants don with the funds collected by the

Director will help him in approving plans that meet the ensuing

industry needs but are always within the law outlined in the

Dairy Council Act. Serving the industry and the Director in this

way is satisfying to me and, like virtually all board members,

I consider it a "labor of love" rather than one returning

immediate economic benefit.

To be required to file a complete financial statement

for me and for my company to serve only in an advisory capacity

WESTERN

3100 SOUTH GRAND AVENUE - LOS ANGELES CALIFORNIA 90007   RICHMOND 9-3204


L.P. Randol Lowenstein                    -2-


Would be time-consuming, and a invasion of my privacy and a

questionable business practice that would serve no public or

private benefit.

Notwithstanding conflict of interest, it would seem that

a body other than those within the dairy industry would find

it most difficult to advise the Air Polution about the use of funds

allotted from the [illegible]. It can well be a Director

into embarrassing decisions that would cause the termination

of programs that are designed to help California's major one

industry - agriculture. A specific example concerns the re-

search efforts the dairy industry, through Dairy Council,

has underwritten to UC Davis for 30 years. The benefit to

the consumer is a better quality product at a lower price in

relation to other time periods. This research must have the

input of the industry to be practical and eventually usable

so that it benefits the public.

I consider my service on the Dairy Council Board to

be of public interest as well as valuable to the dairy

industry. And would like to continue, but not if the govern-

ment makes demands like those listed here.

                                        Sincerely

                                        [illegible] Doyle
                                        Huell Doyle
